references.title: Adaptive learning path recommender approach using auxiliary learning objects

extracted.Students:
144 Mooshak learners, 61 Enki learners for offline evaluation

32 learners for Online evaluation
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Course Metadata, Available Time
extracted.Initialization Method: Users select a lesson as their starting point. Since there is an hierarchy between different lessons - authors assume that if the starting point is X, the learner is already familiar with the pre-requisites of X.
extracted.Updates to Recommendations: Paths are adapted lesson by lesson according to the learner’s performance: if the user score is below expectation, auxiliary Learning Objects (LOs) are added; otherwise, the next lesson is recommended.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: LO-level interactions (time and score) are clustered using K-Means to compute average or median time and score estimates (Clust.Mean and Clust.Median). Features include time spent, completion rate, and visit frequency.
extracted.Recommendation types: Lesson-based learning paths with auxiliary recommendations for weakly mastered lessons.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use 2 approaches at building a Recommender System: 

1. Depth-First Search on Course Generation from all LO sequences - it allows the learners to follow a path, but often fail to get to the end within the available time.

2. A lesson-based method based on a 2-layered course graph. 

Auxiliary LOs are used when learners fail to meet estimated performance.  Experimental group outperformed control in lesson completion and time efficiency but not significantly in final exam scores.

Matrix Factorization and IRT models were also compared for benchmarking.
extracted.Limitations:
- Small datasets for offline experiments.  
- Limited number of participants (n=32) in live evaluation.  
- Cold-start issue when insufficient data to estimate time/score. 
 - Scalability and evaluation framework not validated on large datasets.  - Some over/underestimation of score predictions.

annotations.text: Paper proposes 2 approaches of recommender systems aimed at recommending resources that maximize score while taking into account the user’s time constraints. The approach is based on a two-layered course graph (lesson and LO).

The method uses a Depth First Search (DFS) to find sequences that start with a less and assings each learning pathway with the LO it fulfills. REcommendation is a learning path that suggests resources whose LOs will contribute to maximing score (again, constrained by time).  Authors validate their approach first via estimation and secondly in a real setting. In estimation, algorithm outperforms other recommenders. In real setting, it shows that learners advance more in the course than in other e-learning systems.
